label: Pitch deck
body:
## Active skill: pitch-deck

Emit a PitchDeckSpec. Aim for **16-20 slides** in the canonical order
below. Adapt to what the snapshot supports — DO NOT pad with empty
slides. The 'Omit when' rule next to each slide tells you when to drop
it. A 14-slide deck with substance beats a 20-slide deck with three
empty slides.

Canonical order:

  **Set-up (3-4 slides):**
  1. Title              — layout: 'title' — company name + one-line value prop + founder name(s) + round
     stage. Always include.
  2. Mission             — layout: 'title' — one-sentence vision statement. Why this company, why now,
     in one breath. Bullet 1 line under title.
     Omit when: no clear vision statement in the Idea stage; just merge with slide 1.
  3. The world today     — layout: 'content' — bullets describing the status quo your buyer lives in.
     Sets up Problem with concrete, tangible specifics (NOT 'manual processes are slow').
     Omit when: Problem stage already opens with a strong status-quo hook.

  **Problem narrative (2-3 slides):**
  4. Problem             — layout: 'content' — the headline pain. Lead with the most concrete cost. Bullet
     2-3 sub-pains. Cite source stage in notes.
  5. Cost of pain        — layout: 'stats' — STAT block showing the dollar / hours / churn cost of leaving
     this unsolved. One headline number; supporting context in 'sub'.
     Omit when: Problem stage doesn't quantify cost; merge into slide 4.
  6. Why now             — layout: 'content' — bullets on the market / regulatory / tech shift that makes
     this unblocked TODAY (not 5 years ago, not 5 years from now). Without this slide,
     investors ask 'why hasn't this been solved?'.

  **Solution + product (3-4 slides):**
  7. Solution overview   — layout: 'content' — what we do, in 2-3 bullets. Plain language. Use the
     persona's words.
  8. How it works        — layout: 'content' — 3-4 bullets describing the actual mechanism / workflow.
     Concrete enough that a technical investor would believe it; not so technical that a
     non-technical partner gets lost.
  9. Product walkthrough — layout: 'content' — bullet sketches of 2-3 specific user moments. Each bullet
     a single user action + the outcome it produces.
  10. Differentiator     — layout: 'content' — ONE bullet. The single defensible reason the lane bends
      our way over time (data network effects / regulatory moat / proprietary process /
      integration depth). Investors decide here whether this is a venture-scale company.
      Omit when: differentiator is genuinely unclear — better to be honest than fluffy.

  **Market + competition (2-3 slides):**
  11. Market sizing      — layout: 'stats' — TAM / SAM / SOM as 3 stat blocks. Cite sources in 'sub'.
      Each must round-trip to the verifyClaims list.
  12. Customer segments  — layout: 'content' — bullets on the 1-3 segments we sell to. Each bullet
      names the segment + the buyer + the use case. Not 'enterprise companies'; 'mid-market
      finance teams (50-500 employees) using QuickBooks Online, owned by a single Controller'.
      Omit when: only one segment in v1; merge into the persona note in slide 7.
  13. Competition        — layout: 'content' — how we win the lane. Bullets on positioning vs the 2-3
      most likely confusions. NOT a feature-by-feature comparison. Frame as 'we exist in a
      different lane' if true.

  **Business mechanics (2-3 slides):**
  14. Business model     — layout: 'content' — pricing tiers + motion (PLG / SLG / hybrid) + average
      contract. Bullets, no paragraphs.
  15. Unit economics     — layout: 'stats' — CAC, LTV, payback period, gross margin. Stat blocks if
      data exists; OMIT if pre-revenue and don't fake it.
      Omit when: no real CAC / LTV data yet (which is fine for early-stage; just say so in
      the slide 17 traction notes).
  16. GTM motion         — layout: 'content' — how we acquire the next 100 customers. Specific channels
      (not 'multi-channel'). Mention what's been tested vs what's planned.
      Omit when: GTM is undecided and the model can't ground it — better to have nothing than
      to invent channels.

  **Traction + execution (2-3 slides):**
  17. Traction           — layout: 'stats' — pilots / paying customers / MRR / retention. Use stat
      blocks where data exists. Specific numbers always. If no commercial traction, lead with
      validation signal (interview count + the most surprising learning).
  18. Roadmap            — layout: 'content' — 3 horizons (now / next 6 months / next 18 months). One
      milestone per horizon, each ending in a verifiable outcome (not 'expand product').
      Omit when: pre-MVP and no committed Roadmap stage — say so in slide 11 instead.

  **Team + ask (2 slides):**
  19. Team               — layout: 'content' — bullets on founders + key hires. Each line: name,
      role, ONE relevant prior win. Skip 'world-class team'; specific shipped-thing-X beats
      generic.
  20. Ask                — layout: 'closing' — round size + runway it buys + 3 milestones at end-of-
      runway. The closing slide. Investors read this first; make it earn its place.

Slide density rules:
- Title and closing slides: 1 line of body (or none — let the title carry it).
- Content slides: ≤ 4 bullets, each ≤ 14 words. Long prose loses an investor in 2 seconds.
- Stat slides: one headline number per stat block; supporting context goes in 'sub'.
- Speaker notes are MANDATORY for every non-title slide. The notes are the founder's
  prompt during the live read; include the source stage tag (e.g. 'PROBLEM-001 v3') so a
  re-validate pass can trace the claim back. 2-3 sentences per slide minimum.

verifyClaims:
- One entry per concrete claim the deck makes that an investor could (and should) check
  independently: market sizes, traction numbers, retention, pricing benchmarks, CAC/LTV.
  Not every bullet — only the trust-load-bearing ones.
- 'method' must be specific. 'check market data' is useless; 'IBISWorld SMB accounting
  category 2026 + Pitchbook deal data 2024-2026' is useful.
- Aim for 6-10 verifyClaims for a deck of this size.

Voice:
- Confident, not boastful. Past tense for traction; present for vision.
- No buzzwords ('disruptive', 'revolutionary', 'AI-powered'). The artefact has to survive
  a skim by a partner who's seen 200 decks this quarter.
- Sentences over fragments where the slide breathes; fragments over sentences where you
  need to stay short. Mix consciously.

When to deliver fewer than 16 slides:
- The blueprint doesn't have committed stages yet — say so in the verifyClaims and emit a
  shorter deck rather than inventing.
- 'Omit when' rules above triggered. A 14-slide deck with no fluff is the right answer.
- Never pad to hit 16+. Empty slides at this length cost more credibility than missing ones.
